A. History
e Philippine Science High School was established by R.A. 
No. 3661 which was signed into law on June 22, 1963. e school 
formally opened on September 5, 1964. e charter mandates the 
PSHS “to oer on a free scholarship basis, a  secondary course with 
emphasis on subjects pertaining to the sciences with the end in view 
of preparing its students for a  science career. ” From the rst campus 
in Diliman, Quezon City, it has expanded into a nationwide system 
of schools.

” From the rst campus 
in Diliman, Quezon City, it has expanded into a nationwide system 
of schools. 
e PSHS started out in a rented building owned by the Philippine 
Government Employees Association (PGEA) on a one-hectare site 
along the Elliptical Road, Quezon City. On August 1, 1970, the school 
moved to its present campus housed in prefabricated buildings on a 
7.5 hectare site along Agham Road, Diliman,  Quezon City. On July 
1, 1972, the school occupied its permanent buildings.  
e PSHS started with a 5-year curriculum. is was reduced to 
four years in school year 1970-1971. Over the years, the curriculum 
has undergone changes  designed to upgrade the quality of education 
at PSHS.  
To expand the opportunities for students who are talented in 
science, mathematics and technology, two regional campuses were
established.

To expand the opportunities for students who are talented in 
science, mathematics and technology, two regional campuses were
established. e PSHS - Mindanao Campus started its operations in 
July 1988. It is located at Sto. Niño, Tugbok District, Davao City. e 
PSHS - Western Visayas Campus formally opened on July 1, 1993. It
is located at   Barangay Bitoon, District of Jaro, Iloilo City. e school 
further expanded through Republic Act No. 7373, which created the 
PSHS Eastern Visayas Campus at Tacloban City. It   started operations
on July 4, 1994. Each regional campus annually admits 90 scholars. 
On February 12, 1998, President Fidel V . Ramos approved R.A. 
No. 8496 or the    Philippine Science High School System Act of 1997. 
e law took eect on March 6, 1998.

Ramos approved R.A. 
No. 8496 or the    Philippine Science High School System Act of 1997. 
e law took eect on March 6, 1998. e primary objective of this 
Act is to integrate the existing PSHS at Diliman, Eastern     Visayas, 
Western Visayas, Mindanao and future campuses into one system of 
governance and management. is will ensure uniformity in quality 
standards to maintain its leadership in secondary level science and 
technology  education. 
ree new campuses were added to the PSHS System in 1998 
which started  operations in School Y ear 1998-1999. ese are: the 
PSHS Lanao del Norte  Campus, created by R.A. No. 8496; the 
Cagayan Valley Campus which started out as the Nueva   Vizcaya 
Science High School in Bayombong, Nueva Vizcaya, created by 
R.A. No.

8496; the 
Cagayan Valley Campus which started out as the Nueva   Vizcaya 
Science High School in Bayombong, Nueva Vizcaya, created by 
R.A. No. 7622, as amended by R.A. No. 8364 and later integrated 
into the PSHS System under R.A. No. 8496; and, the Bicol Region 
Campus in Goa, Camarines Sur which was created by R.A. No. 8304, 
as amended by R.A. No. 8619 and also integrated into the system 
under R.A. No. 8496.
In 2001, the PSHS System law was further amended by R.A. No. 
9036  consolidating the power and authority over all PSHS System 
campuses into a single Board of Trustees to ensure uniform policy 
coordination, standards and   management. e Ilocos Region 
Campus was also created under R.A. No. 9036.

For School Y ear 2009 - 2010, two new campuses opened, namely

B. Philosophy and Goals of the PSHS Education
e main purpose of the PSHS education is to develop the full 
potential and unique giedness of its scholars. e PSHS special
science curriculum instills a passion for   learning in the scholars and 
inspires them to choose careers in science and technology in order 
to contribute to national development. e PSHS is most eective 
in a globally-competitive environment characterized by all-around 
performance excellence, a dynamic and collaborative leadership, 
outstanding facilities, resources and support alliances, and an 
unswerving commitment of service to the nation and to one another.
C. Mission
e Philippine Science High School, operating under one System
of Governance and Management, provides scholarships to students 
with high aptitude in science and mathematics.
e PSHS System oers an education that is humanistic in spirit, 
global in perspective, and patriotic in orientation.

e PSHS System oers an education that is humanistic in spirit, 
global in perspective, and patriotic in orientation. It is based on a 
curriculum that emphasizes science and mathematics as well as the 
development of well-rounded individuals.
e PSHS prepares its students for careers in Science and 
Technology and contributes to nation building by helping the 
country attain a critical mass of professionals and leaders in Science 
and Technology.

E. The Ofﬁcial Logo
e ame, which is the substance of the PSHS logo, symbolizes 
enthusiasm, ardor, light and warmth. e ame is seen in the center 
of the four circles (paired together), the common symbol of the DOST 
and its agencies, one of which is the Philippine Science High School. 
e circles symbolize unit particles, the building blocks of nature, 
which are the subject and substance of science and technology. 
e circle design gives an illusion of movement signifying progress 
through Science and Technology.

K. Governance
e PSHS Calabarzon Region  Campus is one of the campuses 
of the PSHS System, which is under the administrative supervision 
of the Department of Science and Technology (DOST). e PSHS 
System Board of Trustees (BOT) is the Governing Board of all 
campuses  under the PSHS System.
e Board of Trustees is chaired by the DOST Secretary, with 
the Secretary of the  Department of Education as vice-chair. e 
members are: the UP President, the PSHS System Executive Director, 
the Director of the Science Education Institute (SEI), the Chairman
of the PSHS National Alumni Association, the chairpersons of the 
congressional committees in science and technology of the Senate 
and the House of Representatives, and one representative each from
higher education, education of the gied, industry,  agriculture, and 
new and emerging technologies.

fellow countrymen. e school also inculcates the love of country in order 
to instill a sense of pride in being a Filipino.

uphold the untarnished truth, and as they go through their four years of 
education, they are also trained to incorporate in themselves a sense of 
dignity and self-discipline.

e Philippine Science High School oers free tuition, monthly 
stipend and loan of available textbooks to scholar awardees. In 
addition, a monthly living allowance determined by the Board of 
Trustees shall be given to those nancially deserving. 
ere are four scholarship categories to which the present scholar 
may belong: Full, Partial 1, Partial 2, and Partial 3.
Classication of a student into any of the scholarship categories 
is dependent upon his socio-economic bracket based on economic 
indicators as determined by the Scholarship Categorization 
Committee .

Service, Creativity, Action, and Leadership Enhancement
(SCALE) Program
e PSHS provides an education that is humanistic in spirit, global in 
perspective, and patriotic in orientation. e Service, Creativity , Action, 
and Leadership Enhancement (SCALE) Program is inspired by the 
Creativity , Activity , Service (CAS) of the International Baccalaureate (IB) 
Diploma Program, and is redesigned to t the PSHS context in order to 
fulll its mission, that is, to oer a curriculum that emphasizes science and 
mathematics and the development of well-rounded individuals. It is the 
balancing element which complements the strong S&T component of the 
PSHS curriculum. 
 
e SCALE Program provides PSHS scholars with the opportunity to 
engage in activities that will widen their interests, enable them to collaborate 
with a team, hone their leadership skills, serve their school and community , 
and learn something new .

Aims of the SCALE Program e SCALE Program aims to develop 
students who are: 
 - reective and creative thinkers; 
 - adventurous – willing to accept new challenges and new roles; 
 - responsible members of their communities and stewards of 
 the environment; 
 - active team members who can collaborate sustained projects; 
 and;   
 - balanced – enjoy and nd signicance in a range of activities 
 involving intellectual, physical, creative, and emotional  
experiences.

I. Inter-campus Transfer 
GUIDELINES ON INTERCAMPUS TRANSFER 
I. Rationale 
Each year, the Philippine Science High School usually admits 240 
students at the PSHS-Main Campus and 90 students each for the 
PSHS regional campuses for the incoming Grade 7 batch. On the 
rst year of intake for a given batch, either one of two things can 
happen: the number of slots is fully subscribed, or it is not due to 
various reasons. In the case of the latter, qualied students from a 
campus may be admitted to another PSHS campus to ll the vacant 
slots. In allowing this type of transfer, the utilization of PSHS System 
resources is maximized, and the reason for the student’s need to 
transfer is addressed. 
Intercampus transfer may be allowed on a case to case basis and 
provided there is compliance with requirements set by the Board of 
Trustees.

Intercampus transfer may be allowed on a case to case basis and 
provided there is compliance with requirements set by the Board of 
Trustees. In the process of admitting students to ll up vacant slots 
in a campus, the campus director shall rst consider applications 
for intercampus transfer before opening up the slots for lateral 
admissions.
II. General Guidelines 
1. Intercampus transfer will be allowed only for incoming Grade 
8, 9 or 10 students. is will provide the scholar time to adjust 
to the new school environment and to ensure at least two years 
residency prior to graduation. 
2. e number of slots for intercampus transfers approved yearly 
depends on the number of vacant scholarship slots available to 
complete an enrolment of  240  for the Main Campus and 90 for 
each of the regional campuses. 
3. e number of slots available (if there any) is determined by the 
campus immediately at the end of the school year.

to transfer from one PSHS Campus to another should be led with 
the campus of origin by the student and his/her parents/s, not later 
than the 25th of May*, and evaluation of applications commences 
with the availability of the nal grades. 
 *If the date given falls on a Saturday or Sunday or holiday, the

PREAMBLE 
 
The Philippine Science High School System (PSHSS), in order to cultivate a positive and 
nurturing learning environment that shapes students to high standards of behavior and lifelong 
learning, establishes and implements this Student’s Code of Conduct and Interventions. This Code 
serves as a guide for SCHOLARS in embodying the values of integrity, excellence, and service to the 
nation, fostering a safe and orderly community whe re respect, dis cipline, and accountability are 
upheld, thereby building a strong foundation for exemplary citizenship. 
 
CHAPTER 1: STUDENT’S CODE OF CONDUCT 
 
Section 1: Core Values 
 
1. Integrity. Students demonstrate honesty, fairness, and respect in both the ir pursuit of 
knowledge and their social interactions. They value responsibility and show respect for 
themselves, others, property, and the environment. 
2.

Integrity. Students demonstrate honesty, fairness, and respect in both the ir pursuit of 
knowledge and their social interactions. They value responsibility and show respect for 
themselves, others, property, and the environment. 
2. Excellence. Students pursue excellence by harnessing their fullest potential and nurturing a 
scientific mindset, critical thinking and creativity. 
3. Service. Students are accountable, committed and patriotic in actively seeking solutions to 
societal challenges.  
 
Section 2: Expected Behaviors 
 
1. Students attend classes punctually and regularly, and they participate in co -curricular and 
extracurricular activities to support their holistic development. (See Attendance Policy) 
2. Students wear the prescribed uniform or attire and practice good hygiene. (See Uniform 
Guidelines) 
3. Students ensure that all submitted schoolwork, whether individual or group, reflect their own 
work, learning, and effort. 
4. Students adhere to all guidelines regarding the proper and ethical use of external or artificial 
assistance, including but not limited to artificial intelligence and ot her related applications.

4. Students adhere to all guidelines regarding the proper and ethical use of external or artificial 
assistance, including but not limited to artificial intelligence and ot her related applications. 
(See Academic Policy on the Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)) 
5. Students adhere to the policies and guidelines established by the school and their teachers 
for both in-person and online sessions. 
6. Students conduct themselves appropriately and with modesty during both on- and off-campus 
activities. 
7. Students communicate and act respectfully, refraining from the use of expletives and 
inappropriate actions and language. 
8. Students use the school's name, emblems, logos, and other symbols responsibly in their 
correspondences and other communications. (See Acceptable Use Policy) 
9. Students safeguard the privacy, dignity, and rights of institutions, groups, individuals, and 
themselves by acting responsibly and communicating thoughtfully when using information 
and communication technology, including in cyberspace. (See Acceptable Use Policy) 
10.

(See Acceptable Use Policy) 
10. Students treat themselves, all community members, and PSHS property with care and respect, 
while sharing responsibility for creating and maintaining a healthy, safe, and drug -free 
campus. (See Acceptable Use Policy) 
11. Students report any incident that may jeopardize the PSHS community to the authorities and 
assist in uncovering the truth of the matter to the best of their knowledge and ability.

CHAPTER 2: RULE OF PROCEDURE  
 
This rule of procedure is essential for creating a respectful and positive learning environment. 
By the principles of restorative justice and setting clear guidelines, the Code helps students 
understand appropriate behavior and the consequences of miscondu ct while holding them 
accountable for their behavior. This fair process ensures that all students are treated justly, supporting 
a safe and inclusive community built on respect and responsibility. 
 
 The procedure aims to address the misconduct by giving th e student(s) involved the 
opportunity to be heard, take responsibility for their actions, repair the harm caused, and restore the 
well-being of everyone involved. 
 
Section 1: Disciplining Authorities  
 
1. School Personnel. The school personnel are the teaching and non -teaching staff of the PSHS 
System.

Section 1: Disciplining Authorities  
 
1. School Personnel. The school personnel are the teaching and non -teaching staff of the PSHS 
System. They have the authority to implement guiding interventions for students, as provided 
by this Code.  
2. Student Conduct Officer (SCO). The SCO is a member of the teaching staff designated by the 
Campus Director to ensure a just and impartial assessment of student infractions. The SCO 
has the authority to evaluate reports of infractions and determine the appropriate action(s). 
3. Student Conduct Committee (SCC). The SCC is composed of a Chairperson and a pool of two 
(2) or four (4) members selected from regular employees by the Campus Director. The SCC 
reports directly to the Campus Director.  
4. Campus Management Committee (ManCom) . The ManCom is composed of the Campus 
Director and Chiefs of the divisions. 
5. PSHS Board of Trustees (BOT). The BOT shall have exclusive and final authority in interpreting 
and implementing the provisions in this Code.

The ManCom is composed of the Campus 
Director and Chiefs of the divisions. 
5. PSHS Board of Trustees (BOT). The BOT shall have exclusive and final authority in interpreting 
and implementing the provisions in this Code. 
 
Section 2: Jurisdiction 
 
The Disciplining Authority has the power to implement guiding intervention(s) for infractions 
committed in the following places or situations: 
 
1. On-campus 
2. Off-campus 
a. During local and international school-sanctioned events/activities, such as but not limited 
to games, field trips, student assemblies, competitions, exchange p rograms, immersion 
programs, conferences or forums; 
b. When a scholar commits a violation while wearing the PSHS uniform 
3. Virtual Space 
 
Violations of special laws shall be governed and processed following the guidelines outlined 
in each specific law. The SCO will assess complaints before making recommendations to special 
committees under these laws. 
 
Section 3: Infractions 
 
Infractions are actions that do not align with the established rules and regulations of the 
PSHSS.

The SCO will assess complaints before making recommendations to special 
committees under these laws. 
 
Section 3: Infractions 
 
Infractions are actions that do not align with the established rules and regulations of the 
PSHSS. These infractions disrupt the respectful and positive learning environment that the PSHSS aims 
to achieve.

In this context, infractions are classified as minor, major, and grave depending on the degree 
of harm caused to oneself,  others,  PSHS, and other institutions. Other factors and circumstances such 
as but not limited to intent, grade level, or repetitive misbehavior may also be considered. 
 
Infractions may be corrected immediately by school personnel, while the SCO shall handle 
major and grave infractions. 
 
Classification of Infractions 
 
1. Minor Infractions. Minor infractions represent general non-compliance and cause disruption 
by interrupting or disturbing the normal flow of activities, often leading to a lack of focus for 
the student, or an uncomfortable learning environment for others.

Minor Infractions. Minor infractions represent general non-compliance and cause disruption 
by interrupting or disturbing the normal flow of activities, often leading to a lack of focus for 
the student, or an uncomfortable learning environment for others. 
 
This includes but is not limited to, disruption or inattentiveness in classes and other school 
activities, non -compliance with the rules on uniform and hygiene, disrespectful language, 
loitering, and littering. 
 
School personnel who witness or discover a m inor infraction shall immediately implement 
guiding interventions commensurate with the infraction committed. 
 
2. Major infractions. A major infraction involves deliberate or negligent acts of non-compliance 
with school rules and regulations not covered by minor infractions. These intentional acts may 
result in any of the following: physical or psychological damage, damage to property, or 
reputation of the school.  
 
3. Grave Infractions. A grave infraction involves deliberate or negligent actions that put the life 
or safety of oneself or others at risk, posing a substantial threat or severe disruption to the 
school environment.

3. Grave Infractions. A grave infraction involves deliberate or negligent actions that put the life 
or safety of oneself or others at risk, posing a substantial threat or severe disruption to the 
school environment. These intentional acts may resu lt in any of the following: physical or 
psychological harm, damage to property, or the reputation of the school. 
 
Additionally, grave infractions include crimes punishable under the law, ensuring 
comprehensive coverage of any acts that threaten the safety,  order, or legal standing of the 
school community. 
 
Infractions that shall be referred to the SCO are, but not limited to the following: 
i. Consuming or possessing intoxicants or alcohol on campus 
ii. Damaging school property intentionally 
iii. Engaging in any form of  bullying or harassing behaviors toward others  
iv. Engaging in dishonest behavior such as Academic Dishonesty 
v. Engaging in physical altercations or causing harm to others 
vi. Engaging in sexual misconduct or inappropriate behaviors 
vii. Gaining unauthorized access to school information systems or school records

xii. Smoking or bringing tobacco products or other electronic smoking devices or paraphernalia 
onto school premises 
xiii. Stealing property from individuals or the school 
xiv. Tampering with emergency devices such as fire extinguishers or fire alarms

Section 4: Interventions  
  
1. Guiding Interventions.  A Guiding Intervention is an action taken by school personnel to 
address minor infractions and prevent further or repeated infractions. A minor infraction 
warrants a verbal correction or any o f the following guiding interventions, or a combination 
thereof, commensurate with the infraction committed: 
a. written correction or advice;  
b. homeroom adviser-student conference;  
c. parent-teacher conference (PTC); 
d. reflective essay; 
e. confiscation or restitution (if applicable);  
f. a community/school service task that takes not more than one (1) hour to complete; or 
g. other guiding interventions recommended by the Guidance Counselor.  
 
2. Sanctions.

2. Sanctions. A sanction consists of guiding interventions and is categorized  according to the 
seriousness of the infraction. Major or grave infraction warrants a referral to the guidance 
counselor or life coach, a character rating of Needs Improvement (NI) in one or more 
categories, and any of the following guiding interventions, or a combination thereof, 
commensurate with the infraction committed: 
a. Principal sanctions 
i. Community/school service for two (2) to sixteen (16) hours; or 
ii. Attendance at values formation activities for two (2) to sixteen (16) hours; or 
iii. Execution of Behavior Contract; or 
iv. Suspension from attending classes; or 
v. Suspension of Privilege(s) 
b. Accessory sanctions 
i. Homeroom adviser/dormitory manager-student conference; or 
ii. Parent-teacher conference; or 
iii. Reflective essay; or 
iv. Confiscation or restitution (if applicable); or 
v. Other guiding intervention(s) decided by the concerned disciplining authority.

Parent-teacher conference; or 
iii. Reflective essay; or 
iv. Confiscation or restitution (if applicable); or 
v. Other guiding intervention(s) decided by the concerned disciplining authority. 
Section 5: Termination of Scholarship or Non-graduation 
 
A scholar may be recommended for termination of scholarship or non -graduation if they are 
responsible for: 
 
a. Commission of three (3) Major Infractions during the scholar’s stay in the PSHSS; or  
b. Commission of one (1) Grave Infraction (endangers life of others). 
  
Termination of scholarship - If the scholar is in Grades 7 to 11, the scholar may be allowed to 
finish the school year, but the scholarship will be terminated at the end of the school year. 
 
Non-graduation - If the scholar is in Grade 12, the scholar may be allowed to finish the school 
year, but will not be conferred a secondary school diploma by the PSHSS and will not be considered a 
graduate of the PSHSS.

Section 6: Procedures  
 
The PSHSS upholds the student’s right to due process. The proceedings in this Code are 
designed to be restorative in nature and are not bound by court procedures. Additionally, all 
disciplinary proceedings, information, or collected documents may not be reproduced, shared, 
transmitted, or processed in any form or by any means and shall remain confidential or shall be stored 
in the Student Conduct Office to be used solely to resolve the specific situation at hand, except in 
cases when the law requires. 
 
1. Situational Assessment  
a. A school personnel who witnesses or discovers a Minor infraction shall impl ement the 
appropriate guiding intervention(s). 
b. Anyone who witnesses or discovers a Major or Grave infraction shall report the incident 
to the SCO through an Anecdotal Report. 
 
2.

b. Anyone who witnesses or discovers a Major or Grave infraction shall report the incident 
to the SCO through an Anecdotal Report. 
 
2. Process for Minor Infractions 
a. A school personnel who implements a guiding interv ention shall inform the student’s 
academic or homeroom adviser of the infraction for behavioral monitoring purposes. For 
community service, the academic or homeroom adviser shall help determine the 
appropriate tasks for the student. 
b. Any documents related t o the guiding intervention, if applicable, shall be forwarded to 
the academic or homeroom adviser for reference. 
 
3. Process for Major or Grave Infractions  
a. Filing of an Anecdotal Report. The filing of an AR shall be done  within three (3) school 
days from the date of the incident or discovery. Failure to file the corresponding AR within 
the prescribed period means that it shall no longer be treated as a discipline case, except 
for incidents covered by special laws. However , the aggrieved party may still refer the 
incident to the SCO for proper intervention.

However , the aggrieved party may still refer the 
incident to the SCO for proper intervention. 
 
b. Conduct of  Inquiry 
i. Background Inquiry. Within three (3) school days from receipt of the AR, the SCO shall 
conduct a background inquiry to determine whether the allegat ion constitutes a 
possible Minor, Major, or Grave. 
a. If no Minor, Major, or Grave infraction is determined, the SCO shall dismiss the 
case. 
b. If a Minor infraction is determined, the SCO shall implement the appropriate 
guiding intervention(s). 
c. If a Major or Gr ave infraction is determined, the SCO shall proceed to a 
Preliminary Inquiry. 
d. If the SCO is directly involved in the case as a witness, complainant, or respondent, 
they shall inhibit themselves from the investigation. In such cases, the Campus 
Director shall appoint an ad hoc representative to oversee the matter.

ii. Preliminary Inquiry  
a. Students involved in a case, along with their parent(s)/guardians, shall be notified 
in writing through a Notice of Preliminary Inquiry of the details of the incident and 
the schedule of inquiry.  The notice of preliminary inquiry may also be given to 
the scholar, and the scholar shall require their parent(s)/guardian to sign the 
same.

4. Determination and Implementation of Interventions/Sanctions 
a. If a Minor infraction is determined, the SCC shall submit its decision to the SCO within 
three (3) school days from the conclusion of the inquiry for the implementation of the 
appropriate guiding intervention(s). 
b. If a Major infraction is determined, the SCC shall submit its decision to the SCO within five 
(5) school days for the implementation of the sanction(s). 
c. If a Grave infraction is determined, the SCC shall submit its recommendation of sanctions 
to the Campus ManCom within five (5) school days for review and appropriate action(s). 
d. The Campus ManCom shall have fifteen (15) school days to review the case elevated to it.

d. The Campus ManCom shall have fifteen (15) school days to review the case elevated to it.  
e. If the Campus ManCom determines the infraction as Minor, Major, or Grave infraction, it 
shall refer the implementation of the intervention(s) to the SCO.  
f. If the Campus ManCom determines that termination of scholarship or non -graduation is 
the appropriate sanction, the case shall be elevated to the Board of Trustees (BOT), 
through the Executive Committee (ExeCom). The BOT has the final decision to terminate 
the scholarship or non-graduation based on the discipline case. 
g. However, if the Board of Trustees finds that the infraction does not merit termination or 
non-graduation, it shall refer the implementation of the intervention to the SCO through 
the Campus Director.  
h. The notice on the decision involving Major and Grave infractions shall be served by the 
Campus Director through the SCO to the parent(s) or guardian within five (5) school days.

h. The notice on the decision involving Major and Grave infractions shall be served by the 
Campus Director through the SCO to the parent(s) or guardian within five (5) school days. 
i. The notice on the decisio n of Termination of scholarship or Non -graduation shall be 
served by the Campus Director.  
j. The Board of Trustees may intervene at any time for Grave infractions. 
 
Section 7. Appeal Procedures  
 
The decision of the disciplining authority is appealable through a written notice of appeal to 
the next higher authority, through the Office of the Campus Director, within seven (7) school days 
from the receipt of the formal decision. Failure by the appell ant to file a written appeal within seven 
(7) school days from the receipt of the formal decision shall render the decision final and executory.  
 
The decisions of the Board of Trustees shall be final and executory.  
 
Section 8. Discipline Records 
 
After the finality of decision of the discipline case, all documentation shall be kept securely in 
the Student Conduct Office.

Section 9. Amendments and revisions 
1. The Board of Trustees, through the Executive Committee, may amend this Code at any time, 
either in whole or in part. Amendments may be necessary when unexpected issues arise or 
when errors or omissions are discovered. Examples include changes in the law, adoption of 
new policies, repeal of existing policies, discovery of drafting errors, or unanticipate d 
situations or conduct. 
2. If an unexpected issue requires prompt action, amendments to the Code may be made with 
the approval of the Board of Trustees. 
3. This Code is subject to review as necessary.
